research_question: What is heterogeneity I  threshold for substantial in meta-analysis interpretation? Cite Higgins/Thompson 2003 + Cochrane Handbook current thresholds (low/moderate/substantial/considerable).

findings.claim: The widely cited 2003 methodological paper by Higgins, Thompson, Deeks, and Altman introduced the I2 statistic and tentatively proposed thresholds of 25%, 50%, and 75% to characterize low, moderate, and high heterogeneity, respectively.

Modern EBM guidelines, including the current Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions, have transitioned to using overlapping ranges instead of strict cutoffs, officially defining 50% to 90% as the threshold that may represent 'substantial heterogeneity'.

The current Cochrane Handbook maps the remaining I2 ranges as follows: 0% to 40% (might not be important), 30% to 60% (may represent moderate heterogeneity), and 75% to 100% (considerable heterogeneity), emphasizing that interpretations must consider the magnitude and direction of the intervention effects.

convergence_flags.description: While Higgins et al. (2003) originally proposed fixed, singular cutoffs (25/50/75), the Cochrane Handbook deliberately uses broad, overlapping ranges (e.g., 30-60% and 50-90%) to discourage rigid rule-of-thumb interpretations of I2.
PMID 12958120 (Higgins/Thompson 2003) confirmed real via NCBI esummary: title=Measuring inconsistency in meta-analyses, PubDate=2003 Sep 6

gaps: Thresholds detailed are primarily for meta-analyses of standard clinical interventions; the evidence does not specify whether alternative thresholds are recommended for diagnostic test accuracy (DTA) or network meta-analyses.
